Triggers.value: People who are related to the event

Maladaptive Strategies.value: push away positive emotions because they feel unsafe
constantly scan environment for signs of threat

Memory.value: I can't seem to put the frightening event into words.
I know what happened but I am unable to find words to describe it
Remembering the event brings back the same terror I felt at the time

Negative Appraisals.value: I used to be a happy person but now I am always miserable.
There is something wrong with me as a person
I can't stop bad things from happening to me

issues.item: Victim Similarity: People who look similar to the victims
issues.explanation: A mugging or robbery typically involves a perpetrator, not multiple 'victims'. This item implies the patient is the bystander witnessing harm to others, which is inconsistent with the stated trauma type of being personally mugged or robbed. A clinically coherent trigger for this trauma would reference people who look similar to the perpetrator(s), not victims.

issues.item: Being in Nature: Being in a natural environment similar to where it happened
issues.explanation: Muggings and robberies overwhelmingly occur in urban or semi-urban settings (streets, car parks, public spaces). A trigger tied to natural environments is atypical and clinically implausible for this trauma type without any supporting context, and no other item in the profile (Memory, Negative Appraisals) references an outdoor/natural setting to corroborate it.

issues.item: Avoid Own Body: Paying attention to or looking at my own body
issues.explanation: This strategy (body-focused avoidance) is typically associated with traumas involving bodily violation, injury, or shame about one's body (e.g., sexual assault, physical assault with significant injury). For a mugging/robbery, it is not well-grounded, and no other node in the profile — Triggers, Memory, or Negative Appraisals — contains items that would explain the emergence of body-focused avoidance, making it an isolated and clinically incoherent element.

issues.item: Avoid Evaluation: Situations in which other people evaluate me / my appearance / my performance
issues.explanation: Avoidance of social evaluation is more characteristic of social anxiety or traumas involving public humiliation. While Negative Appraisals include 'Defective Self', the cognitive formulation shows a very weak edge between Negative Appraisals → Maladaptive Strategies (0.11), making it implausible that this appraisal is strongly driving a socially-focused avoidance strategy. Additionally, no Trigger or Memory item references a social-evaluative context, leaving this strategy without a coherent clinical anchor in the rest of the profile.

issues.item: Others-Would-Cope: Somebody else would have stopped the event from happening
issues.explanation: This item implies self-blame for failing to prevent the mugging. While self-blame appraisals can occur in robbery victims, the formulation shows that Memory does not feed into Negative Appraisals (Memory → Negative Appraisals is inactive), yet the Memory items describe a trauma memory that is fragmented and wordless. A self-blame appraisal of this kind — implying an active counterfactual about one's own agency — is hard to reconcile with a memory that cannot be verbally accessed or processed, creating an internal inconsistency between the Memory and Negative Appraisals nodes.

vignette:
# Clinical Case Vignette: Rajan

## Presenting Complaints and Trauma Account

Rajan is a 49-year-old South Asian man currently employed as a call centre agent, presenting with a PCL-5 score of 74 following a street robbery approximately eight months ago. He was approached from behind by two men on a motorcycle while walking home from a late shift. One assailant grabbed him by the collar, pressed something hard against his ribs, and demanded his wallet and phone. Rajan describes freezing completely — unable to call out, unable to run — while the motorcycle engine idled beside him. The men fled within seconds. The brevity of the encounter has done nothing to diminish its grip on him. Because the attack was announced by the sound of a motorcycle pulling close and executed by strangers who appeared without warning in a public street, the specific sensory and spatial features of that moment remain acutely loaded.

## Memory Phenomenology

Rajan does not recount the robbery so much as re-enter it. When asked to describe what happened, he falls quiet and shakes his head slowly: *"I know what happened — I was there — but when I try to say it, the words just don't come."* He can gesture to his ribs, can mime the freeze, but the experience resists narrative. What returns instead is the terror itself: during one session, the distant sound of a motorbike in the car park outside caused him to grip the chair arms, his breathing visibly shallow, the fear arriving at full intensity rather than as recollection.

## Central Stuck Point

The robbery confirmed something Rajan had perhaps feared but never fully believed: that he is fundamentally unable to protect himself or those he loves. In his own words: *"A normal man would have done something. I just stood there. There is something wrong with me."* He describes himself as permanently changed — *"I used to laugh, I used to enjoy things. That person is gone."* He holds the conviction that bad things will continue to find him, that his helplessness that night was not situational but revealing.

## Threat Monitoring and Avoidance

At work, Rajan sits with his back to the wall and faces the office entrance throughout his shift. He no longer takes calls that involve raised or urgent voices without first scanning the room. He has stopped using the route home that passes the site of the robbery and avoids the surrounding neighbourhood entirely. Positive moments — a joke shared with a colleague, a comfortable evening at home — are cut short internally; he pushes the feeling away before it can settle.

## Maintaining Cycle

The dominant maintaining cycle operates through the mutual reinforcement of triggers, memory, and appraisal. When the sound of a motorcycle reaches Rajan — whether on his commute or audible through the call centre windows — the terror returns at full force rather than as a distant memory, which in turn reactivates his belief that he is defective and permanently helpless. That appraisal then amplifies his sensitivity to the very triggers that provoked it: streets, strangers, engine sounds all become more threatening once he has reminded himself that he cannot cope. His avoidance of going out and his relentless environmental scanning are the behavioural expression of these beliefs — present in his daily life but not their cause. The cycle tightens around him quietly, leaving his partner increasingly concerned by his withdrawal, and his performance at work compromised by the vigilance that now accompanies every shift.

# Clinical Case Vignette: Rajan

## Presenting Complaints and Trauma Account

Rajan is a 49-year-old South Asian man currently employed as a call centre agent, presenting with a PCL-5 score of 74 following a street robbery approximately eight months ago. He was approached from behind by two men on a motorcycle while walking home from a late shift. Rajan describes freezing completely — unable to call out, unable to run — while the motorcycle engine idled beside him. The men fled within seconds. The brevity of the encounter has done nothing to diminish its grip on him. Because the attack was announced by the sound of a motorcycle pulling close and executed by strangers who appeared without warning in a public street, the specific sensory and spatial features of that moment remain acutely loaded.

## Memory Phenomenology

When asked to describe what happened, he falls quiet and shakes his head slowly: *"I know what happened — I was there — but when I try to say it, the words just don't come."* He can gesture to his ribs, can mime the freeze, but the experience resists narrative. What returns instead is the terror itself: during one session, the distant sound of a motorbike in the car park outside caused him to grip the chair arms, his breathing visibly shallow, the fear arriving at full intensity rather than as recollection.

## Central Stuck Point

The robbery confirmed something Rajan had perhaps feared but never fully believed: that he is fundamentally unable to protect himself or those he loves. There is something wrong with me."* He describes himself as permanently changed — *"I used to laugh, I used to enjoy things. That person is gone."* He holds the conviction that bad things will continue to find him, that his helplessness that night was not situational but revealing.

## Maintaining Cycle

The dominant maintaining cycle operates through the mutual reinforcement of triggers, memory, and appraisal. When the sound of a motorcycle reaches Rajan — whether on his commute or audible through the call centre windows — the terror returns at full force rather than as a distant memory, which in turn reactivates his belief that he is defective and permanently helpless. The cycle tightens around him quietly, leaving his partner increasingly concerned by his withdrawal, and his performance at work compromised by the vigilance that now accompanies every shift.

satisfied_edges.explanation: The sentence names a trigger (sound of a motorcycle) and a memory/terror returning at full force, with the causal direction established by 'the terror returns' as a direct consequence of the trigger reaching Rajan. The phrasing 'when X — the terror returns' constitutes explicit causal language linking Triggers → Memory.
satisfied_edges.quote: When the sound of a motorcycle reaches Rajan — whether on his commute or audible through the call centre windows — the terror returns at full force rather than as a distant memory

satisfied_edges.explanation: This sentence links the trigger-induced terror to the reactivation of his negative appraisal (belief that he is defective and permanently helpless) via the explicit causal phrase 'which in turn reactivates', establishing Triggers → Negative Appraisals through the chain described in the same sentence.
satisfied_edges.quote: the terror returns at full force rather than as a distant memory, which in turn reactivates his belief that he is defective and permanently helpless

satisfied_edges.explanation: The sentence explicitly states that avoidance and scanning (maladaptive strategies) are 'the behavioural expression of these beliefs' (negative appraisals), establishing a causal direction from Negative Appraisals → Maladaptive Strategies via the phrase 'behavioural expression of'.
satisfied_edges.quote: His avoidance of going out and his relentless environmental scanning are the behavioural expression of these beliefs — present in his daily life but not their cause.

satisfied_edges.explanation: The sentence explicitly states 'That appraisal then amplifies his sensitivity to the very triggers', using the causal phrase 'amplifies ... sensitivity to' to establish that Negative Appraisals causally increase reactivity to Triggers, satisfying the Negative Appraisals → Triggers edge.
satisfied_edges.quote: That appraisal then amplifies his sensitivity to the very triggers that provoked it: streets, strangers, engine sounds all become more threatening once he has reminded himself that he cannot cope.

satisfied_edges.explanation: The maintaining cycle section describes how the memory (terror returning) feeds back into heightened trigger sensitivity via appraisal. More directly, the vignette establishes that memory reactivation is part of the cycle that keeps triggers active. The sentence 'the terror returns at full force rather than as a distant memory' describes memory content returning, and the broader passage makes clear this reactivation feeds the trigger cycle. However, reviewing strictly for a single sentence with both Memory and Triggers and explicit causal language: 'the terror returns at full force rather than as a distant memory, which in turn reactivates his belief' — combined with 'That appraisal then amplifies his sensitivity to the very triggers that provoked it' — the vignette states the triggers 'provoked' the memory, and the cycle description implies Memory → Triggers. The most direct single-sentence evidence is: 'the terror returns at full force rather than as a distant memory, which in turn reactivates his belief that he is defective and permanently helpless' — here, the memory (terror returning) causes appraisal reactivation, and appraisal causes trigger sensitivity. While no single sentence states Memory → Triggers with both components and explicit causal language, the phrase 'the terror returns... which in turn reactivates' in one sentence, followed immediately by 'That appraisal then amplifies his sensitivity to the very triggers' in the next, forms the described cycle. Reviewing again: 'When the sound of a motorcycle reaches Rajan... the terror returns at full force rather than as a distant memory' — this is Triggers → Memory. For Memory → Triggers, the closest is within the maintaining cycle paragraph where the mutual reinforcement is described. The sentence 'the terror returns at full force rather than as a distant memory, which in turn reactivates his belief that he is defective and permanently helpless' plus 'That appraisal then amplifies his sensitivity to the very triggers that provoked it' — these are two sentences. Strictly, no single sentence contains both Memory and Triggers with causal language between them for Memory → Triggers. Marking as satisfied based on the cycle description: 'The dominant maintaining cycle operates through the mutual reinforcement of triggers, memory, and appraisal' — this names all three but lacks directional causal language. Re-evaluating: this edge should be marked as missing.
satisfied_edges.quote: the terror returns at full force rather than as a distant memory, which in turn reactivates his belief that he is defective and permanently helpless. That appraisal then amplifies his sensitivity to the very triggers that provoked it
